skill.name: histolab
skill.description: Lightweight Whole Slide Image (WSI) tiling and preprocessing for digital pathology; use when you need fast tissue detection and tile extraction to prepare datasets or run quick tile-based analysis.
skill.summary: Lightweight toolkit for tiling, masking, and preprocessing Whole Slide Images in digital pathology workflows.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 307 static analysis findings are false positives. The HIGH severity 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are from the term 'HED' (Hematoxylin-Eosin-DAB stain deconvolution, a standard histology concept) being misinterpreted as a cryptographic hash. The MEDIUM severity 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections are markdown code fences (```python) misidentified as shell backticks. The LOW severity 'System reconnaissance' detections are standard WSI property and path references. The only non-zero risk is a hardcoded GitHub source URL which is a legitimate attribution link. This is a documentation-only skill with no executable code.

high_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm misidentified (HED stain term)
high_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static scanner flagged the term 'HED' (Hematoxylin-Eosin-DAB, a standard stain deconvolution color space in digital pathology) as a weak cryptographic algorithm. Also flagged unrelated lines (SKILL.md:3, slide_management.md:5) containing no crypto-related content. No actual cryptographic operations exist in this documentation-only skill.

medium_findings.title: Markdown code fences misidentified as shell backtick execution
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static scanner flagged triple backtick code blocks (```python) throughout all reference markdown files and SKILL.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are standard markdown code fences containing static Python code examples for the histolab library. No actual shell execution occurs.

medium_findings.title: Static Python import misidentified as dynamic import()
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static scanner flagged a standard Python import statement at references/filters_preprocessing.md:306 as a 'Dynamic import() expression'. The actual code is a static Python import (from histolab.filters.morphological_filters import ...) in a markdown code example. No eval or dynamic module loading is present.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance misidentified (WSI property references)
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The scanner flagged references to slide file paths, metadata properties, and pyramid levels as 'System reconnaissance'. These are standard Whole Slide Image processing concepts: accessing slide dimensions, magnification metadata (openslide.objective-power), and file paths. No actual system scanning occurs.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub source URL
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE but LOW RISK: The skill contains a hardcoded URL pointing to its source repository on GitHub (https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills). This is a legitimate source attribution link, not a data exfiltration endpoint. No credentials, tokens, or sensitive data are transmitted.

content.user_title: Process and Analyze Whole Slide Images from Pathology
content.value_statement: Gigapixel pathology slides are too large for direct analysis and require significant preprocessing before they can be used in research. This skill automates tissue detection, tile extraction, and preprocessing using the histolab library to prepare pathology datasets for downstream analysis tasks.
actual_capabilities: Load and inspect whole slide images in SVS, TIFF, and NDPI formats
Detect tissue regions using automatic thresholding and morphological filters
Extract image tiles with random, grid, or score-based selection strategies
Preview mask boundaries and tile locations before running full extraction
Build custom filter pipelines for tissue segmentation and artifact removal
Generate tile location reports with scoring metadata for quality assessment
limitations: Does not perform stain normalization or color correction on extracted tiles
Cannot train, evaluate, or deploy deep learning models on extracted tile datasets
Limited to tile extraction and preprocessing; no built-in diagnostic or classification capabilities
Performance depends heavily on WSI file size, pyramid level, and available system memory

use_cases.title: Prepare pathology datasets for machine learning workflows
use_cases.description: Extract tissue tiles from cancer slide collections to build training datasets for deep learning models. Use ScoreTiler with nuclei scoring to prioritize tiles with high cellular content.

use_cases.title: Process batch slides for clinical trial analysis
use_cases.description: Apply consistent grid-based tile extraction across hundreds of slides with reproducible parameters. Generate tile reports with spatial coordinates and tissue coverage metrics.

use_cases.title: Explore tissue morphology through random tile sampling
use_cases.description: Quickly sample diverse regions from whole slide images to understand tissue composition and staining quality before committing to full analysis pipelines.

prompt_templates.title: Extract random tiles from a pathology slide
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to extract 200 random tissue tiles from a prostate cancer slide at 512x512 resolution. Load the slide, create a tissue mask, and extract tiles with 80% minimum tissue content using a reproducible seed.
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic tile extraction for dataset creation

prompt_templates.title: Cover entire slide with grid extraction
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract non-overlapping grid tiles from a breast tissue whole slide image at level 1 resolution. Set tile size to 256x256 and enable tissue content filtering to skip background areas.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive slide coverage for spatial analysis

prompt_templates.title: Extract top-scoring tiles using nuclei density
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ScoreTiler with NucleiScorer to extract the 50 highest-quality tiles from a colorectal cancer slide. Generate a CSV report with tile names, coordinates, scores, and tissue percentage.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quality-focused tile selection for targeted analysis

prompt_templates.title: Build a custom filter pipeline for artifact removal
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a custom tissue detection pipeline using Compose with RgbToGrayscale, OtsuThreshold, BinaryDilation, and RemoveSmallObjects filters. Apply it to exclude pen marks and artifacts from the extraction area.
prompt_templates.scenario: Custom preprocessing for challenging slide quality

output_examples.input: Load a prostate tissue slide from slide.svs and extract 50 random tissue tiles at 512x512 resolution with 80% minimum tissue content.
output: A thumbnail preview image with mask boundaries overlaid in red is saved to the output directory
A tile location preview image shows 20 randomly sampled tile positions as colored rectangles on the slide thumbnail
50 PNG tile images are saved to output/tiles/ with filenames like tile_001.png through tile_050.png
The output directory also contains a thumbnails subfolder with the slide thumbnail for quick reference

output_examples.input: Apply grid-based extraction to a breast cancer slide with 256x256 tile size, level 1 resolution, and 20% pixel overlap between adjacent tiles.
output: A systematic grid of tiles covering all tissue regions is extracted, with each tile overlapping its neighbors by approximately 51 pixels
Tiles are saved as PNG files with a naming convention reflecting their grid position
Background-only tiles are automatically skipped because tissue content filtering is enabled
The total number of extracted tiles depends on the slide dimensions and tissue coverage area

best_practices: Always preview mask boundaries and tile locations before running a full extraction to verify parameters and avoid wasted computation.
Use appropriate pyramid levels for your analysis: level 0 for maximum detail, higher levels for faster processing when full resolution is not needed.
Set reproducible seeds in RandomTiler and document all tiler parameters to ensure consistent results across experimental runs.
anti_patterns: Extracting tiles without first verifying the tissue mask quality, which can result in datasets dominated by background or artifact regions.
Processing all slides at the highest resolution level (level 0) when lower pyramid levels would provide sufficient detail for the analysis task.
Using default tiler parameters across slides with different stain types or tissue characteristics without adjusting thresholds and filter settings.

faq.question: What WSI file formats does histolab support?
faq.answer: Histolab supports SVS, TIFF, and NDPI formats through the OpenSlide library. It can read most common whole slide image formats used in digital pathology.

faq.question: How do I choose between RandomTiler, GridTiler, and ScoreTiler?
faq.answer: Use RandomTiler for sampling and exploratory analysis, GridTiler for comprehensive slide coverage and spatial analysis, and ScoreTiler for targeted quality-driven extraction based on scoring functions like nuclei density.

faq.question: What does the tissue_percent parameter control?
faq.answer: The tissue_percent parameter sets the minimum percentage of tissue content required for a tile to be accepted. A value of 80 means only tiles with at least 80 percent tissue coverage are kept, filtering out background-heavy tiles.

faq.question: How can I create a custom tissue mask?
faq.answer: Create a custom mask by subclassing BinaryMask and implementing the _mask method, or by passing a custom filter pipeline built with Compose to the existing TissueMask class. This allows you to handle specific staining patterns or artifact types.

faq.question: Why are no tiles being extracted from my slide?
faq.answer: Common causes include: tissue_percent threshold is too high for the actual tissue content, the extraction_mask does not capture the tissue area, tile_size is too large for the slide resolution, or the WSI file may not contain detectable tissue.

faq.question: Does histolab support multi-GPU or distributed processing?
faq.answer: No, histolab is designed for single-process CPU-based tile extraction. For distributed processing of large slide collections, you would need to implement parallel processing across slides manually using Python multiprocessing or a workflow manager.
